content:
Adhesion energy between cell, encapsulant, and glass layers decreases under both hot-dry and hot-humid accelerated aging conditions. Coupon-level testing after 5000 h of exposure at 90°C under ~1% RH (hot-dry) and 60% RH (hot-humid) demonstrated measurable decreases in adhesion energy Gc [REF:5]. The presence of moisture amplifies the degradation pathway: tensile strength of adhesive joints decreased by approximately 31.3% after moisture absorption alone, while combined exposure to high temperature (95°C) and humidity drove losses to 54.4% [REF:6]. These quantitative results converge with kinetic modeling that describes the adhesion degradation rate as proportional to the moisture concentration at the interface, governed by an Arrhenius temperature dependence [REF:3]. Higher temperature increases both the rate of chemical reaction and the diffusion coefficient, promoting greater water penetration and further degradation of adhesive joints [REF:6].

The mechanistic picture emerging from these data distinguishes between reversible and irreversible damage modes. Interface strength reduction, the appearance of microstructure defects, and hydrolysis of the polymer matrix are all irreversible consequences of damp-heat aging [REF:6]. This irreversibility contrasts with the behavior observed under purely thermal cycling. Thermocycling over 10,000 cycles between 5°C and 50°C had no statistically significant effect (p > 0.05) on the interfacial mechanics and fracture mode of either experimental or commercial adhesives [REF:4]. The divergence between moisture-driven and thermally driven outcomes reinforces the kinetic model's emphasis on moisture concentration as the rate-controlling variable at the interface [REF:3][REF:4].

| Aging Condition | Degradation Observed | Source |
|---|---|---|
| Hot-dry, 90°C, ~1% RH, 5000 h | Gc decreased | [REF:5] |
| Hot-humid, 90°C, 60% RH, 5000 h | Gc decreased | [REF:5] |
| Moisture absorption only | Tensile strength −31.3% | [REF:6] |
| Combined 95°C + humidity | Tensile strength −54.4% | [REF:6] |
| Thermocycling, 10,000 cycles, 5–50°C | No significant effect (p > 0.05) | [REF:4] |

Comparative performance across encapsulant chemistries reveals material-dependent degradation rates under damp heat. EVA laminates showed less degradation in peel force after damp heat than POE samples across the tested lamination temperatures [REF:2]. POE laminates, by contrast, revealed improved compressive shear performance after damp-heat aging compared to their unaged state [REF:7]. This apparent divergence—EVA outperforming POE in peel force retention while POE improves in shear—indicates that adhesion degradation kinetics are both material-specific and test-mode dependent [REF:2][REF:7]. Peel strength and shear strength capture different interfacial failure mechanisms, and ranking encapsulants by one metric alone risks mischaracterizing their damp-heat durability.

The kinetic framework linking degradation rate to interfacial moisture concentration and Arrhenius-type temperature dependence [REF:3] provides a unifying model, yet its applicability across all encapsulant families remains unverified. Current quantitative data concentrate on EVA and POE systems [REF:2][REF:7], with adhesion energy measurements on cell/encapsulant/glass configurations [REF:5] and tensile testing on adhesive joints exposed to combined thermo-humid conditions [REF:6]. The absence of equivalent fracture energy (kJ/m²) measurements for TPO or other polyolefin encapsulants under standardized damp-heat protocols represents a significant evidence gap. Similarly, the irreversible nature of hydrolysis-driven degradation [REF:6] raises durability questions that Arrhenius extrapolation from accelerated tests may not fully capture, particularly when the dominant failure mechanism transitions from interfacial debonding to bulk polymer degradation at longer exposure durations.

**Key Findings**

- Tensile strength of adhesive joints decreased by 31.3% under moisture absorption alone and by 54.4% under combined high temperature (95°C) and humidity, confirming moisture as the primary degradation driver [REF:6].
- Adhesion energy Gc decreased after 5000 h of both hot-dry (90°C, ~1% RH) and hot-humid (90°C, 60% RH) aging on cell/encapsulant/glass coupons [REF:5].
- Thermocycling alone (10,000 cycles, 5–50°C) had no statistically significant effect (p > 0.05) on interfacial mechanics, isolating moisture as the critical variable [REF:4].
- EVA laminates retained peel force better than POE under damp heat [REF:2], while POE showed improved compressive shear after aging [REF:7], indicating test-mode-dependent degradation ranking.
- Interface strength reduction, microstructure defects, and polymer matrix hydrolysis are irreversible effects of damp-heat exposure [REF:6].
